content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 17, 2006


Jeffrey G. Scott
General Counsel
Grossmont Healthcare District
16935 West Bernardo Drive, Suite 170
San Diego, CA 92127


RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-06-148

Dear Mr. Scott:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of Mr. Robert P. Yarris a board member of the Grossmont Healthcare District ("Healthcare District"), regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 (the "Act").¹ Because your request seeks general guidance beyond a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit Mr. Yarris from making, participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to a Health Education Center, which may be constructed by the Grossmont-Cuyamaca Community College District ("College District"), considering his employment with the Grossmont-Cuyamaca Community College District Auxiliary Organization ("College Auxiliary")?

CONCLUSION

Mr. Yarris will have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from making, participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to the Health Education Center if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial

_______________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-06-148
Page No. 2

effect on the College Auxiliary, if the nexus test is met, or if there is a personal financial
effect on Mr. Yarris as discussed herein.

FACTS

The Healthcare District is located in La Mesa, California and is organized under
the provisions of the Health and Safety Code sections 32000 et seq., to provide healthcare
services and to operate healthcare related facilities for residents in east San Diego
County.

Mr. Yarris has served on the Healthcare District Board since December 1992. In
addition to his Healthcare District Board position, Mr. Yarris is also an employee of the
College Auxiliary, holding a position as the Program Director of the Regional Health
Occupations Resource Center. As an employee of the College Auxiliary, Mr. Yarris
teaches a class at Grossmont College two to three times a week and provides leadership
and direction to regional community colleges in workforce development. In addition,
Mr. Yarris' work involves the administration and management of all grants and budgets
for the College Auxiliary.

The College Auxiliary is a nonprofit public corporation, which receives grants
from private foundations and contracts with the College District. As an employee of the
College Auxiliary, Mr. Yarris receives no salary or other form of remuneration from the
College District.

The College District and the Grossmont College, which is a college of the College
District, are exploring the construction of a Health Education Center on property owned
by the Healthcare District, which may also include funding from the Healthcare District
for nursing and other healthcare-related education programs.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

File No. I-06-148
Page No. 3

**Step One: Is Mr. Yarris a "public official?"**

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; regulation 18700(b)(1).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency. . . ."
(Section 82048.) As a member of the Healthcare District Board, Mr. Varris is a public
official within the meaning of the Act.

**Step Two: Would Mr. Yarris be making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?**

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening subsequent review, the official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) As a member of the Healthcare District Board, Mr. Yarris would
be making, participating in making, and/or influencing governmental decisions when
considering decisions related to the Health Education Center.

**Step Three: What are Mr. Yarris's "economic interests?"**

There are six kinds of economic interests recognized under the Act. Those that
may be applicable, according to your account of the facts, are the following:

**Business Entity** -- A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in
which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(a) and (d); regulation 18703.1(a) and (b).) However, only businesses
operated for profit are defined by the Act as business entities. (Section 82005.) Under
your account of the facts, the College Authority is a nonprofit organization and the
College District is a government entity. Accordingly, Mr. Yarris does not have an
economic interest in either the College Auxiliary or the College District as a business
entity.

**Sources of Income** -- A public official has an economic interest in any source of
income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.) The facts you have
presented indicate that Mr. Yarris is an employee of the College Auxiliary and receives

File No. I-06-148
Page No. 4

income from the College Auxiliary. If Mr. Yarris receives income from the College
Auxiliary aggregating to $500 or more within 12 months prior to a decision, Mr. Yarris
has an economic interest in the College Auxiliary as a source of income.³

Based upon the facts you have provided, the College Auxiliary is the source of
Mr. Yarris's salary and the College Auxiliary appears to be a source of income to Mr.
Yarris. Moreover, in some circumstances, we have advised that there may be more than a
single source for a payment. (Dorsey, Advice Letter, No. A-87-176.) Considering the
fact that Mr. Yarris teaches at Grossmont College two to three nights a week, there is a
question of whether Mr. Yarris's salary could be attributed to the College District and/or
Grossmont College in addition to the College Auxiliary. While you have not provided
sufficient facts to determine whether the College District and/or the Grossmont College
could also be considered a source of Mr. Yarris's salary, section 82030(b)(2) provides
that salary received from a state, local, or federal agency is not considered income under
the Act. Accordingly, while the College Auxiliary appears to be a source of income to
Mr. Yarris, the College District and Grossmont College are not considered sources of
income to Mr. Yarris, as any salary that could be attributed to the College District and
Grossmont College, which are both governmental agencies, would not be considered
income under the Act.

Personal Financial Effects -- A public official has an economic interest in his or
her personal finances. In particular, a governmental decision has a personal financial
effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities of the official increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; regulation
18703.5.)

Step Four: Are Mr. Yarris's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in
the decision?

Sources of Income:

After identifying all economic interests, it is necessary to determine whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision. Regulation 18704.1(a) states
that a source of income is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency
when that source of income, directly or by an agent:

³ We are not analyzing whether the College Auxiliary could be considered a local governmental
entity based upon the factors provided in the Commission's opinion In re Siegel (1977) 3 PPC Ops. 62.
(See also Hearey Advice Letter, No. A-01-251; Stone Advice Letter, No. A-97-630; and Francis Advice
Letter, No. A-86-214.) While we note that a salary received from a governmental entity is not considered
income under the act (section 82030(b)(2)), you have not provided the facts necessary to make a
determination of whether the College Auxiliary should be considered a local governmental agency and
have provided only that the College Auxiliary is a nonprofit organization. To determine whether
Mr. Yarris's has a disqualifying conflict of interest, Mr. Yarris should consider the salary received from the
College Auxiliary income and the College Auxiliary a source of income unless additional facts establish
that the College Auxiliary should be considered a local governmental entity.

File No. I-06-148
Page No. 5

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."

Because you have not identified a specific governmental decision and have only
generally asked whether Mr. Yarris can make, participate in making, or influence
the process and decisions related to the Health Education Center, we cannot determine
whether the College Auxiliary is directly involved in any specific governmental decision.
If you need additional assistance determining whether Mr. Yarris's economic interest in
the College Auxiliary is directly or indirectly involved in any specific governmental
decision, it is advisable that you seek further assistance, providing all relevant facts.

Personal Financial Effects:

If facts suggest any financial effect on Mr. Yarris's personal finances,
Mr. Yarris's economic interest in his personal finances is deemed to be directly involved
in the governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.5.)

Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on Mr. Yarris's economic interest(s)?

Materiality

Sources of Income:

Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.

For economic interests in nonprofit organizations, directly involved in a decision,
which are sources of income to an official, the materiality standard is given at regulation
18705.3(a) which states, "Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is
a source of income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before
the official's agency, is deemed material."

For economic interests in nonprofit organizations, indirectly involved in a
decision, which are sources of income to an official, the materiality standard is given at
regulation 18705.3(b) (copy enclosed). The materiality thresholds in this regulation vary
with the size of the nonprofit organization. Because you have not provided sufficient

File No. I-06-148
Page No. 6

information for us to calculate the materiality threshold for Mr. Yarris's economic
interest in the College Auxiliary, we must leave the calculation up to Mr. Yarris.
Mr. Yarris should consult 18705.3(b) to ensure that he identifies the materiality threshold
actually appropriate to the College Auxiliary.

In addition, there is a separate and distinct materiality standard that applies in cases
where there is a "nexus" between duties owed to a source of income and to the official's
public agency. The materiality threshold is understandably much lower when a public
official is paid to accomplish some action that is within the official's public decision-
making authority. Under the nexus rule, "Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a
person who is a source of income to a public official is deemed material if the public
official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be
achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision." (Regulation 18705.3(c).)

The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to
accomplish a purpose that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official, we
presume the employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her official
capacity. (Yarnell Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.) Typically, a "nexus" is found in
situations where the official is also a high-level employee with direct influence and control
over his or her employer's management or policy decisions. (Moser Advice Letter, No.
A-03-147; Low Advice Letter, No. A-99-304.)

You stated that Mr. Yarris is the College Auxiliary's Program Director for the
Occupations Resource Center. In this capacity, Mr. Yarris provides leadership and
direction to regional community colleges in workforce development, is involved in the
administration and management of all grants and budgets for the College Auxiliary, and
teaches two to three nights a week at Grossmont College. As a Program Director,
Mr. Yarris's tasks may include promoting governmental action, which would help to carry
out the College Auxiliary's mission. Thus, a nexus may exist, and a material financial
effect may occur, if a particular governmental decision in which a high-level administrator
like Mr. Yarris is asked to participate would also achieve, defeat, aid or hinder a policy,
purpose, or goal of College Auxiliary. (See Moser, supra; Rankin Advice Letter, No. A-
94-310.)

You have not presented enough facts for us to analyze fully whether the nexus test
is met. Accordingly, Mr. Yarris must consider whether the decisions relating to the Health
Education Center will achieve a goal or purpose, which will have a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on the College Auxiliary, which he is paid to bring about. If a nexus is
found, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the College Auxiliary from a
governmental decision relating to the Health Education Center, even a penny's worth, is
deemed material.

File No. 1-06-148
Page No. 7

Personal Financial Effects:

Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in regulation
18705.5, which provides in pertinent part:

"(a) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public
official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250
in any 12-month period."

Foreseeability

Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standards will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on the facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.

Because you have not identified a specific governmental decision nor provided
any facts regarding the foreseeability of the potential financial effects on the College
Auxiliaries or Mr. Yarris's personal finances, we cannot determine whether there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Mr. Yarris's economic interests.
However, if Mr. Yarris determines that there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on one or more of his economic interests with respect to a specific governmental
decision, he may apply the materiality standards as set forth above. Should you need
further assistance determining if a disqualifying conflict of interest exists, we suggest that
you request additional assistance, providing all relevant facts as they relate to a specific
governmental decision.

Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?

Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; regulation 18707(a).)

File No. 1-06-148
Page No. 8


Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from
acting. (Section 87101; regulation 18708.)

You have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to Mr. Yarris's circumstances,
so we will not address them further.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure

regulation 18705.3(b) (copy enclosed). The materiality thresholds in this regulation vary

QUESTION: Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit Mr. Yarris from making,
participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to a Health
Education Center, which may be constructed by the Grossmont-Cuyamaca Community
College District ("College District'), considering his emplo5rment with the Grossmont-
Cuyamaca Community College District Auxiliary Or ganiz ation ("College Auxiliary,,)?

CONCLUSION: Mr. Yarris will have a conflict of interest disqualifizing him from making,
participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to the Health
Education Center if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
I Government Code sections 8 1000 - gl}14. ; rggulation 18329(c)(3).)
rrygl
effect on the College Auxiliary, if the nexus test is met, or if there is a personal financial
effect on Mr. Yarris as discussed herein.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 August 17, 2006 Jeffrey G. Scott General Counsel Grossmont Healthcare District 16935 West Bernardo Drive, Suite 170 San Diego, CA 92127 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-06-148 Dear Mr. Scott: This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of Mr. Robert P. Yarris a board member of the Grossmont Healthcare District ("Healthcare District"), regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974 (the "Act").¹ Because your request seeks general guidance beyond a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² QUESTION Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit Mr. Yarris from making, participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to a Health Education Center, which may be constructed by the Grossmont-Cuyamaca Community College District ("College District"), considering his employment with the Grossmont-Cuyamaca Community College District Auxiliary Organization ("College Auxiliary")? CONCLUSION Mr. Yarris will have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from making, participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to the Health Education Center if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial _______________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3).) File No. I-06-148 Page No. 2 effect on the College Auxiliary, if the nexus test is met, or if there is a personal financial effect on Mr. Yarris as discussed herein. FACTS The Healthcare District is located in La Mesa, California and is organized under the provisions of the Health and Safety Code sections 32000 et seq., to provide healthcare services and to operate healthcare related facilities for residents in east San Diego County. Mr. Yarris has served on the Healthcare District Board since December 1992. In addition to his Healthcare District Board position, Mr. Yarris is also an employee of the College Auxiliary, holding a position as the Program Director of the Regional Health Occupations Resource Center. As an employee of the College Auxiliary, Mr. Yarris teaches a class at Grossmont College two to three times a week and provides leadership and direction to regional community colleges in workforce development. In addition, Mr. Yarris' work involves the administration and management of all grants and budgets for the College Auxiliary. The College Auxiliary is a nonprofit public corporation, which receives grants from private foundations and contracts with the College District. As an employee of the College Auxiliary, Mr. Yarris receives no salary or other form of remuneration from the College District. The College District and the Grossmont College, which is a college of the College District, are exploring the construction of a Health Education Center on property owned by the Healthcare District, which may also include funding from the Healthcare District for nursing and
